name: Pelliceira Climb
name_original: Subida a Pelliceira – ida y vuelta desde San Antolín de Ibias
description: This difficult 19.3-mile road cycling route, the Ascent to Pelliceira, offers challenging climbs and stunning mountain views from San Antolí
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Ibias traverse the mountainous terrain of the Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias Natural Park in Asturias, Spain. The region is characterized by sparsely populated mountain ranges, deep valleys, and extensive forests, including significant beech and oak woodlands. Road cyclists encounter winding roads, challenging ascents, and descents through a landscape shaped by rivers and glacial formations, offering varied and demanding rides. Best road cycling routes around Ibias The most popular road cycling route is Pelliceira Climb, a 19.3 miles (31.1 km) trail that takes 2 hours 21 minutes to complete. This route features a significant elevation gain, making it a challenging ascent through the mountainous landscape. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Ancares Pass loop from A Proba, a difficult 45.7 miles (73.6 km) path. This loop includes substantial climbs and descents, offering a demanding ride through high mountain passes. Local road cyclists also love the O Cebreiro – Cebreiro Pass loop from A Proba, an 83.4 miles (134.2 km) trail leading through high mountain passes and valleys, often completed in about 8 hours 0 minutes. Road cycling around Ibias is defined by challenging mountain ranges, picturesque valleys, and extensive forests. The network primarily offers routes for experienced cyclists seeking significant elevation gains and long distances. The routes in Ibias are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 reviews. More than 223 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ibias's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the typical terrain like for road cycling in Ibias?
A: Road cycling in Ibias primarily involves mountainous terrain within the Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias Natural Park. You can expect winding roads, challenging ascents, and exhilarating descents through deep valleys and extensive forests. The region is characterized by sparsely populated mountain ranges like Degaña and Rañadoiru, offering varied and demanding rides.

Q: Are there many challenging road cycling routes in Ibias?
A: Yes, Ibias is known for its challenging road cycling routes. Out of 20 available routes, 19 are classified as difficult, featuring significant elevation gains and long distances. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40303040" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;O Cebreiro – Cebreiro Pass loop from A Proba&lt;/a&gt; is an 83.4-mile trail with over 3,300 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see while road cycling in Ibias?
A: While road cycling in Ibias, you'll encounter a rich variety of natural features. The region is part of a Biosphere Reserve, boasting extensive forests, including significant beech and oak woodlands. You'll ride through picturesque valleys shaped by rivers like the Ibias and Narcea, and may even glimpse glacial formations in areas like the Cueto de Arbás reserve, which features lagoons and peatlands.

Q: Are there any notable viewpoints or passes along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the mountainous landscape of Ibias offers several notable passes and viewpoints. You can find highlights such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5593878" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo de las Mujeres Muertas Pass&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5593872" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Connio Pass&lt;/a&gt;, and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4524571" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Acebo Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. These spots provide stunning panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and valleys.

Q: When is the best time of year to go road cycling in Ibias?
A: The best time for road cycling in Ibias is generally during the warmer months, from late spring to early autumn. This period offers more stable weather conditions, making the challenging mountain passes and winding roads more accessible and enjoyable. The extensive forests also provide shaded routes during hotter days.

Q: Are there any family-friendly or easy road cycling routes in Ibias?
A: Ibias is predominantly characterized by challenging road cycling routes, with most classified as difficult. There are currently no easy routes listed, and only one moderate route out of 20. The terrain is generally suited for experienced cyclists seeking significant elevation gains and long distances, rather than beginners or families with young children.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in Ibias?
A: Yes, there are several circular road cycling routes available in Ibias. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38171428" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ancares Pass loop from A Proba&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 45.7-mile loop that includes substantial climbs and descents through high mountain passes. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40303040" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;O Cebreiro – Cebreiro Pass loop from A Proba&lt;/a&gt;, which is an 83.4-mile trail.

Q: Where are good starting points for road cycling routes in Ibias, and is parking available?
A: Many road cycling routes in Ibias, particularly the longer loops, often start from villages or towns within or near the Natural Park, such as A Proba. While specific parking information for every starting point isn't detailed, these smaller communities typically offer some parking options. For routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31715499" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roadbike loop from Centro recepción de Muniellos&lt;/a&gt;, starting points are often near known facilities or attractions.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Ibias?
A: The road cycling experience in Ibias is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the challenging mountain ranges, the quiet roads, and the spectacular natural beauty of the region, including its picturesque valleys and extensive forests. The demanding ascents and exhilarating descents amidst clean air are frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any cultural attractions or points of interest near the cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can find cultural points of interest. The region is home to an interpretation center in San Antolín de Ibias dedicated to the palloza, a traditional dwelling of the area. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6276468" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muniellos Interpretation Center&lt;/a&gt; is another facility that provides insight into the local environment and culture.

Q: What are the road conditions like for cycling in Ibias?
A: The roads in Ibias, particularly within the Natural Park, are generally quiet and well-maintained, offering a pleasant cycling experience. However, given the mountainous terrain, expect winding roads with varying surfaces, and be prepared for ascents and descents that require good road cycling skills. The region's commitment to preserving its natural environment ensures clean air and scenic routes.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Ibias?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes available in Ibias, offering a wide range of options for enthusiasts. These routes primarily cater to experienced cyclists due to their challenging nature, featuring significant elevation changes and substantial distances.
tips: Rolling around Cecos, on the border with Degaña, the parish of Cecos is a transit area for the beautiful climb up to Pelliceira.

Bordering Galicia, the Asturian Parish of Pelliceira is located at an altitude of 950 meters and about 15 km from San Antolín de Ibias. For road bike fans, this climb offers us a drop of 866 meters with a length of 11.10 km, an average % of 7.80 and a maximum slope of 16%.

faqs.question: What type of bike is recommended for the Ascent to Pelliceira route?
faqs.answer: Given the significant unpaved sections, particularly on both approaches to Pelliceira and towards the summit, a gravel bike or a robust road bike equipped with wider tires is highly recommended. Traditional road bikes may struggle with the gravel segments.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route features a mix of challenging paved sections with significant gradients and substantial unpaved gravel segments. You'll encounter steep climbs, especially on the Coro-Rio Balouta side (averaging 8.1% with sections up to 15.5%) and the Cecos side (averaging 7.1% with sections up to 17%). The final 2.6 km after Pelliceira Village are entirely on gravel.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult due to its significant elevation gain (over 1,000 meters), challenging gradients, and the presence of unpaved gravel sections. It requires very good fitness and experience with demanding climbs.

faqs.question: Where does the route start, and what are the parking options in San Antolín de Ibias?
faqs.answer: The route is a round trip starting and ending in San Antolín de Ibias. While specific parking details aren't provided, San Antolín de Ibias is a small town, and you should be able to find suitable street parking or small public parking areas within the village to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience stunning mountainous landscapes characteristic of the border region between Asturias and Galicia. The route passes through charming settlements like Cecos Village and Pelliceira Village. Expect breathtaking views of the steep Cantabrian Mountains, deep valleys, and rolling green landscapes, reminiscent of the Pyrenees or Dolomites.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the warmer, drier months to avoid adverse weather conditions on the challenging climbs and gravel sections. Spring and early autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and clearer skies, making for a more enjoyable and safer experience.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no special permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Ascent to Pelliceira route. It traverses public roads and trails.

faqs.question: Is the Ascent to Pelliceira route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily for cycling, if you were to consider walking sections, dogs are generally allowed on public trails in this region. However, given the challenging nature of the route and the presence of gravel, it might not be ideal for all dogs, especially if you plan to cycle the entire distance. Always keep dogs under control, preferably on a leash, and clean up after them.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the [CIMA AS16] Pelliceira, Ruta dos Ancares, A Faena, Senda del Oro, and [CIMA AS03] Connio * Cecos.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and remote nature, it's essential to bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit for your bike (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate cycling gear for varying weather conditions, and a fully charged phone. A GPS device with the route loaded is also highly recommended due to the challenging terrain and potential for unpaved sections.

faqs.question: What makes the Ascent to Pelliceira a notable cycling destination?
faqs.answer: The Ascent to Pelliceira is notable for its extremely challenging gradients, its location on the border of Asturias and Galicia, and the significant gravel sections that distinguish it from purely paved climbs. It offers a true adventure for cyclists seeking demanding mountain passes and breathtaking views in a region renowned for its world-class cycling opportunities.
